---
title: "3.5 Methods: How to Write Them"
subtitle: "Part 2: Getters and toString"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Public methods expose selected information

Because instance variables are usually `private`, outside code cannot read them directly.

An <span class="term">accessor</span> or <span class="term">getter</span> method provides controlled read access.

```java
public String getName()
{
    return name;
}
```

## Getter Pattern

### Return type matches the data

```java
public int getGradeLevel()
{
    return gradeLevel;
}
```

Typical getter features:

::: {.tight-list}
- `public`
- non-`void`
- usually no parameters
- returns one piece of object state
:::

## Code Task

### Student getters

```java
public class Student
{
    private String name;
    private int gradeLevel;

    public Student(String studentName, int studentGrade)
    {
        name = studentName;
        gradeLevel = studentGrade;
    }

    public String getName()
    {
        return name;
    }

    public int getGradeLevel()
    {
        return gradeLevel;
    }
}
```

Call with `student.getName()` or `student.getGradeLevel()`.

## Why Getters Matter

### Encapsulation with controlled access

Getters keep instance variables private while still allowing other classes to use needed information.

```java
Student s = new Student("Mina", 10);
System.out.println(s.getName());
```

Outside code asks the object for information through a method. It does not directly touch `name`.

## `toString`

### Return a readable object description

`toString` returns a `String` representation of an object.

```java
public String toString()
{
    return name + " is in grade " + gradeLevel;
}
```

It is especially useful for printing, tracing, and debugging.

## Code Task

### Add `toString`

```java
public class Student
{
    private String name;
    private int gradeLevel;

    public Student(String studentName, int studentGrade)
    {
        name = studentName;
        gradeLevel = studentGrade;
    }

    public String toString()
    {
        return name + " is in grade " + gradeLevel;
    }
}
```

`System.out.println(student);` can use this method automatically.

## Quick Check

### Getter or `toString`

Choose the better method for each need:

::: {.tight-list}
- Need only the student's name: `getName()`
- Need a printable summary of the whole object: `toString()`
- Need the grade as an `int`: `getGradeLevel()`
:::

Good class interfaces expose useful information without exposing every variable directly.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain why private instance variables need public accessors
- write a getter with a matching return type
- explain why getters usually have no parameters
- write a `toString` method that returns a readable `String`
- distinguish a single-value getter from an object summary
:::

## End

### Continue or return to the course outline

[Next: Setters and Parameters](3-5-part-3-setters-and-parameters.html)  
[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
